summary: Julia Evans describes her second attempt at learning Rust, finding it dramatically easier than her first try in 2013. She credits both the language's maturation and a new learning strategy of pair programming with an experienced Rust developer, letting him handle the parts she didn't understand. She argues Rust is easier to learn than C for systems programming, noting that common tasks like hashmaps and string formatting are surprisingly approachable. She highlights Rust's helpful error messages, good documentation, and compatibility with tools like valgrind and perf.
key_points: Rust has matured significantly since 2013 and now feels closer to writing Python than struggling with a systems language
Pair programming with an expert and selectively ignoring complexity you don't yet understand is an effective learning strategy
Common programming tasks (hashmaps, regex, string formatting) are far easier in Rust than in C, making it a better entry point for systems programming
Rust's error messages, official documentation, and investment in developer experience (like paying Steve Klabnik to write docs) make a real difference
Rust programs work seamlessly with systems tools like valgrind and perf, with debug info available for profiling
money_quotes: I think he's right that learning Rust is easier than learning C.
Those things would have been really hard in C (how do you even make a hashmap??? I think you have to write the data structure yourself or something.).

I kind of enjoy the experience of having a Magical Oracle to fix my programming problems for me – having someone elide away the harder stuff so I can focus on what's easy feels to me like a good way to learn.
Of course, you can't let someone else fix all your hard programs forever.
But for today I only understand like 6 things and that's fine.
Sometimes they link to GitHub issues, and someone on the GitHub issue will have a workaround for your problem!
key_insight: You don't need to understand everything about a language to start being productive in it — selectively ignoring complexity and relying on experts for the hard parts is a valid and effective learning strategy.
